title: redcore OS Playbook
shortDescription: Windows optimization playbook for redcore OS
description: A review-first Windows optimization playbook that stages cleanup, privacy hardening, and performance tuning through a guided wizard.
details: redcore OS scans the current install, resolves a profile-aware plan, and packages the same decisions for wizard and ISO flows.

bulletPoints.title: Lower Latency
bulletPoints.description: Stages the same timer, power, and scheduler optimizations exposed by the desktop wizard.

bulletPoints.title: Telemetry Control
bulletPoints.description: Removes the worst consumer tracking defaults without pretending every enterprise feature can disappear.

bulletPoints.title: Rollback Ready
bulletPoints.description: Keeps rollback metadata so the plan can be audited and reversed instead of acting like a blind tweak pack.

desktopQuestions.label: Transformation Depth
desktopQuestions.title: How hard should redcore OS push this machine?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the mainline preset. It decides how much of the playbook is eligible before your per-question answers start forcing things in or out.
desktopQuestions.note: Conservative is for caution. Balanced is for most tuned personal machines. Aggressive and Expert expose deeper latency/security tradeoffs.

options.title: Conservative
options.desc: Focus on cleanup, basic privacy reduction, and low-risk ergonomics. Leaves the deeper scheduler, power, and protocol questions out.

options.title: Balanced
options.desc: The serious default. Enables meaningful performance and privacy questions without drifting into enthusiast-only territory.

options.title: Aggressive
options.desc: Unlocks deeper latency, networking, and power-management questions meant for tuned personal rigs.

options.title: Expert
options.desc: Unlocks mitigation and HVCI questions along with the highest-risk browser/network/security tradeoffs.

desktopQuestions.label: Power Plan
desktopQuestions.title: Activate the Windows High Performance power plan?
desktopQuestions.desc: This forces Windows away from the default power-saving bias so the CPU stops dipping as aggressively between bursts.
desktopQuestions.note: This is the most defensible baseline tweak from both enthusiast guides and real-world tuning work.

options.title: Yes — use High Performance
options.desc: Better responsiveness and less ramp-up delay under game and desktop load.

options.title: No — keep Windows power defaults
options.desc: Safer for mixed-use or battery-conscious machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows balanced power behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: CPU Scheduler
desktopQuestions.title: Apply foreground-biased thread priority tuning?
desktopQuestions.desc: This maps to Win32PrioritySeparation. It pushes the active foreground workload ahead of background noise.
desktopQuestions.note: redcore OS only asks this question at the level it can actually apply today.

options.title: Yes — bias CPU time toward the active app
options.desc: Useful for games and latency-sensitive foreground work.

options.title: No — keep Windows scheduler defaults
options.desc: Better if you prefer untouched desktop multitasking behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default Windows thread scheduling behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Timer Behavior
desktopQuestions.title: Restore global timer-resolution requests on supported Windows builds?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the real Windows 11 timer question redcore OS can honestly apply: whether the system should honor global timer-resolution behavior again.
desktopQuestions.note: This is not a fake fixed 0.5 ms timer lock. It applies the real GlobalTimerResolutionRequests path in the playbook.

options.title: Yes — restore global timer behavior
options.desc: Lets one process' timer request benefit the whole system again.

options.title: No — keep modern per-process timer behavior
options.desc: More power-efficient and closer to stock Windows scheduling.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep modern per-process timer behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Search Indexing
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Search indexing?
desktopQuestions.desc: Indexing keeps background I/O and CPU pressure around so Start and Explorer search stay fast. Turning it off trades convenience for quietness.
desktopQuestions.note: This is the measurable part of the old “search removal” culture that actually exists in the playbook.

options.title: Yes — disable indexing
options.desc: Reduces background disk churn and CPU spikes from the indexer.

options.title: No — keep indexing enabled
options.desc: Better if you actively use Start or file search.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search indexing enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Search Web Noise
desktopQuestions.title: Strip web results, search highlights, history, and the oversized taskbar search box?
desktopQuestions.desc: This cuts the Microsoft content layer around Search without pretending to fully remove SearchHost.
desktopQuestions.note: This is intentionally more honest than claiming complete search removal when the actual playbook applies targeted shell/search actions.

options.title: Yes — shrink Search to a local-only utility
options.desc: Cleaner Start/Search surface with less suggestion spam and history noise.

options.title: No — keep Windows Search extras
options.desc: Best if you like built-in web suggestions or richer search panels.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search web features and history behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Printing
desktopQuestions.title: Does this machine still need printer support?
desktopQuestions.desc: If yes, redcore OS will preserve the Print Spooler. If no, it will cut it.
desktopQuestions.note: This question is preservation-driven: the answer directly decides whether the spooler disable action is blocked or applied.

options.title: Yes — preserve printer support
options.desc: Keeps the Print Spooler and avoids surprise printer breakage.

behavior.blockReason: You said this machine still needs printer support.

options.title: No — safe to disable printing services
options.desc: Good for stripped gaming machines with no local or network printers.

desktopQuestions.label: Remote Access
desktopQuestions.title: Does this machine still need RDP, remote assistance, or support tools?
desktopQuestions.desc: If yes, redcore OS preserves remote-access related services. If no, it trims that attack surface.
desktopQuestions.note: This is the kind of preservation decision that should be explicit before apply, not discovered after something breaks.

options.title: Yes — preserve remote access capability
options.desc: Safer for workstations, remote support, or self-hosted remote access workflows.

behavior.blockReason: You said this machine still needs remote access or remote support.

options.title: No — disable remote access services
options.desc: Good for personal machines that should never accept inbound remote control.

desktopQuestions.label: Browser Surface
desktopQuestions.title: How hard should Edge background behavior be suppressed?
desktopQuestions.desc: This question stays on the honest side: preload, nags, and updates are separable; full browser removal is asked separately.
desktopQuestions.note: Suppressing Edge is usually the sweet spot. Removing Edge is a different risk class and comes later.

options.title: Keep Edge unchanged
options.desc: No preload, nag, or update suppression.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Microsoft Edge behavior unchanged.

options.title: Suppress background preload and default-browser nags
options.desc: Cuts startup boost and self-promotion without freezing browser updates.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to freeze Edge updates.

options.title: Suppress Edge and also freeze auto-updates
options.desc: Adds update suppression on top of preload/nag cleanup. Highest maintenance burden short of full removal.

desktopQuestions.label: AI Surface
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Copilot?
desktopQuestions.desc: This removes the Copilot taskbar surface and its associated shell noise.

options.title: Yes — remove Copilot from the shell
options.desc: Cleaner taskbar and less AI-first push from Windows.

options.title: No — keep Copilot available
options.desc: Leave the Copilot surface intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Copilot available.

desktopQuestions.label: Recall
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Recall?
desktopQuestions.desc: Recall is one of the easiest “yes” questions in the whole flow unless you explicitly want AI timeline snapshots.
desktopQuestions.note: Only shown on supported Windows builds where Recall actually exists.

options.title: Yes — disable Recall
options.desc: Cuts screenshot-history style AI capture behavior entirely.

options.title: No — keep Recall available
options.desc: Only sensible if you actually intend to use it.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Recall available.

desktopQuestions.label: Click To Do
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Click to Do?
desktopQuestions.desc: This trims another Windows 11 AI shell surface that most tuned machines do not need.
desktopQuestions.note: Only shown on supported Windows builds where Click to Do is present.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Less shell clutter and fewer AI-driven hooks.

options.title: No — keep it available
options.desc: Leave the new interaction surface intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Click to Do available.

desktopQuestions.label: AI In Apps
desktopQuestions.title: Disable AI features in Edge, Paint, and Notepad?
desktopQuestions.desc: This cuts the bundled AI surfaces in Microsoft apps rather than only the headline shell features.

options.title: Yes — disable app-level AI features
options.desc: Removes AI extras from the apps most people never asked to become assistants.

options.title: No — keep those AI features
options.desc: Leave the AI tooling in those apps available.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AI features in system apps.

desktopQuestions.label: Telemetry Depth
desktopQuestions.title: How far should Microsoft telemetry reduction go?
desktopQuestions.desc: This controls the real telemetry actions in the playbook rather than vague “privacy mode” marketing.
desktopQuestions.note: Aggressive adds SIUF/feedback suppression on top of the standard telemetry, CEIP, and error-reporting cuts.

options.title: Keep Windows telemetry behavior
options.desc: Do not apply telemetry-specific privacy cuts here.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to reduce Microsoft telemetry in this area.

options.title: Reduce telemetry
options.desc: Disable telemetry, CEIP, and error reporting without going all the way into the aggressive feedback cuts.

behavior.blockReason: You chose the lighter telemetry reduction profile.

options.title: Aggressive telemetry reduction
options.desc: Adds deeper feedback suppression for users who want Microsoft’s soft collection surfaces cut harder.

desktopQuestions.label: Clipboard History
desktopQuestions.title: Disable clipboard history and cross-device clipboard sync?
desktopQuestions.desc: Useful if you want the clipboard to behave like a simple local buffer again.

options.title: Yes — disable clipboard history/sync
options.desc: Cuts cloud-linked clipboard behavior and local history retention.

options.title: No — keep clipboard history
options.desc: Leave clipboard convenience features enabled.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep clipboard history and sync capability.

desktopQuestions.label: Activity Feed
desktopQuestions.title: Disable activity feed and cloud-content style tracking?
desktopQuestions.desc: This trims the timeline-style “what you were doing” surfaces and associated cloud content behavior.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Less behavior tracking and less cloud-fed content inside Windows.

options.title: No — keep that behavior
options.desc: Leave Windows activity and cloud-content behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep activity feed or cloud content behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Location
desktopQuestions.title: Disable location services and Find My Device?
desktopQuestions.desc: A good privacy answer for desktops. A more situational one for laptops that actually use location-based workflows.

options.title: Yes — disable location-linked features
options.desc: Reduces location tracking and disables Find My Device dependence.

options.title: No — keep location available
options.desc: Better if you use maps, recovery, or location-aware apps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows location-aware features.

desktopQuestions.label: Personalization Tracking
desktopQuestions.title: Disable tailored experiences, online tips, and app-launch tracking?
desktopQuestions.desc: This removes the “Windows learns from you” layer instead of only the obvious ad toggles.

options.title: Yes — cut that personalization layer
options.desc: Less suggestion spam and less behavior-based tuning from Windows.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Leave Windows’ softer personalization layer intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep tailored experiences and app-launch personalization.

desktopQuestions.label: Speech & Input
desktopQuestions.title: Disable online speech and input-personalization telemetry?
desktopQuestions.desc: This cuts cloud speech/input collection behavior that most stripped systems do not need.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Reduces speech/input data collection and personalization layers.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Better if you rely on cloud speech features or dictation behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speech and input personalization features.

desktopQuestions.label: SmartScreen
desktopQuestions.title: Disable SmartScreen reputation checks?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is a real tradeoff question. It reduces Windows friction, but it also removes one of the last browser/download guardrails people forget they were relying on.
desktopQuestions.note: Unlike marketing privacy toggles, this one can genuinely increase risk if the machine is used casually.

options.title: Yes — disable SmartScreen
options.desc: For stripped personal rigs where you intentionally manage trust yourself.

warnings: Disabling SmartScreen can remove a useful download and reputation safety check.

options.title: No — keep SmartScreen
options.desc: Safer for mixed-use, browsing, and non-lab machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SmartScreen protection enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Fast Startup
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Fast Startup?
desktopQuestions.desc: Fast Startup often leaves stale driver/kernel state around. Tuned systems usually benefit from clean boots.

options.title: Yes — disable Fast Startup
options.desc: Cleaner shutdown/boot behavior and fewer weird resume-state driver bugs.

options.title: No — keep Fast Startup
options.desc: Faster boots, but more hybrid-shutdown baggage.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fast Startup enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Hibernation
desktopQuestions.title: Disable hibernation?
desktopQuestions.desc: For most tuned desktops this is a yes. For laptops it depends on whether you actually use hibernate.

options.title: Yes — disable hibernation
options.desc: Frees disk space and keeps boot behavior cleaner.

options.title: No — keep hibernation
options.desc: Leave hibernate available as a power feature.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep hibernation enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Audio Enhancements
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows audio enhancements?
desktopQuestions.desc: DSP and enhancement layers are often needless overhead unless you intentionally rely on them.

options.title: Yes — turn enhancements off
options.desc: Cleaner, more predictable audio path with less vendor/DSP interference.

warnings: Audio enhancements off can change loudness, EQ, and motherboard vendor DSP behavior.

options.title: No — keep enhancements
options.desc: Better if you rely on loudness EQ, virtual surround, or motherboard DSP effects.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows audio enhancements enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Exclusive Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Enable exclusive-mode audio control?
desktopQuestions.desc: This helps users who want a cleaner low-latency audio path and are okay with apps taking exclusive control of the device.

options.title: Yes — enable exclusive mode
options.desc: Better for low-latency or focused audio paths when the app supports it.

options.title: No — keep the current shared-mode behavior
options.desc: Safer if you constantly juggle multiple apps using the same device.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to enable exclusive-mode audio control.

desktopQuestions.label: Context Menu
desktopQuestions.title: Restore the classic full Windows 11 context menu?
desktopQuestions.desc: A pure usability question, but one with a real apply-side effect.

options.title: Yes — restore the classic menu
options.desc: Gets rid of the compact extra-click Windows 11 context menu.

options.title: No — keep the stock menu
options.desc: Leave the modern compact menu in place.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default Windows 11 compact context menu.

desktopQuestions.label: Taskbar End Task
desktopQuestions.title: Add End Task to taskbar right-click menus?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the exact Windows 11 usability tweak you asked for: kill hung apps straight from taskbar right-click without opening Task Manager.
desktopQuestions.note: Only shown on Windows builds that expose the taskbar End Task toggle.

options.title: Yes — add End Task
options.desc: Cleaner emergency-kill path for frozen apps.

options.title: No — keep default taskbar right-click behavior
options.desc: Leave the standard Windows 11 menu intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default taskbar right-click behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Background Apps
desktopQuestions.title: Disable background app execution?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is a real resource-control question. It matters more than a lot of flashy “FPS tweaks.”

options.title: Yes — stop background app execution
options.desc: Cuts silent background UWP/app activity.

options.title: No — keep background apps allowed
options.desc: Better if you rely on synced app behavior or background refresh.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep background app execution enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Automatic Maintenance
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows automatic maintenance?
desktopQuestions.desc: This trades background housekeeping for a quieter machine. Good for dedicated tuned PCs; less good for people who want Windows to self-maintain.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Fewer surprise background maintenance runs while idle.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Safer if you want Windows doing its normal housekeeping.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows automatic maintenance enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Game Mode
desktopQuestions.title: Force Game Mode on?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the lightweight Windows-native gaming toggle, not a magical optimization. But if you game, it still belongs in the questionnaire.

options.title: Yes — enable Game Mode
options.desc: Lets Windows bias scheduling a bit more around games.

options.title: No — keep current Game Mode behavior
options.desc: Leave Windows to its current default behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to force Game Mode on.

desktopQuestions.label: Transparency
desktopQuestions.title: Disable transparency effects?
desktopQuestions.desc: Small tweak, but real. Cuts acrylic/blur visual noise and a bit of compositor overhead.

options.title: Yes — disable transparency
options.desc: Cleaner, flatter shell with fewer translucent effects.

options.title: No — keep transparency
options.desc: Leave the Windows visual style intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows transparency effects enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Boost Policy
desktopQuestions.title: Use aggressive processor boost mode?
desktopQuestions.desc: This keeps the CPU pushing harder under load instead of backing off into friendlier boost policy behavior.

options.title: Yes — use aggressive boost
options.desc: Higher performance bias and quicker sustained turbo behavior.

options.title: No — keep stock boost policy
options.desc: Safer thermally and calmer for mixed-use machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to force aggressive CPU boost behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Minimum CPU State
desktopQuestions.title: Lock minimum processor state to 100%?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the “don’t dip clocks between bursts” question. It is one of the cleaner CPU responsiveness levers in the current playbook.

options.title: Yes — keep the floor at 100%
options.desc: Reduces clock-drop latency between bursts.

options.title: No — allow Windows to downscale more freely
options.desc: Better for thermals and general-purpose efficiency.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to lock minimum processor state to 100%.

desktopQuestions.label: Core Parking
desktopQuestions.title: Disable CPU core parking?
desktopQuestions.desc: Classic enthusiast latency question. Useful on many desktop gaming systems, less sensible on weaker or office-centric machines.

options.title: Yes — unpark cores
options.desc: Reduces wake-up latency when Windows suddenly needs more threads.

options.title: No — keep core parking behavior
options.desc: Better for efficiency-biased or cooler-running setups.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep CPU core parking behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: MMCSS
desktopQuestions.title: Apply the tuned MMCSS gaming profile?
desktopQuestions.desc: This hardens the scheduler profile used for Games under Windows’ multimedia scheduling system.

options.title: Yes — apply the tuned gaming profile
options.desc: Useful for keeping game/media threads favored over background noise.

options.title: No — keep MMCSS defaults
options.desc: Leave multimedia scheduling closer to stock.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to harden the MMCSS gaming profile.

desktopQuestions.label: Memory Compression
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows memory compression?
desktopQuestions.desc: A sensible question on machines with enough RAM. On RAM-starved systems, the answer can be different.

options.title: Yes — disable memory compression
options.desc: Less CPU spent compressing memory pages; best on healthy-RAM systems.

options.title: No — keep memory compression
options.desc: Safer if you want Windows to defend capacity under pressure.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows memory compression enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: GPU Scheduling
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Hardware-Accelerated GPU Scheduling (HAGS)?
desktopQuestions.desc: HAGS is system-specific. The point here is not blind faith, it is making the choice explicit.

options.title: Yes — disable HAGS
options.desc: Often chosen for steadier behavior on tuned systems.

options.title: No — keep HAGS at the Windows default
options.desc: Leave GPU scheduling behavior untouched.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep HAGS at the Windows default.

desktopQuestions.label: GPU Telemetry
desktopQuestions.title: Disable AMD/NVIDIA telemetry services?
desktopQuestions.desc: Cleans out the always-on driver analytics layer without pretending the GPU driver itself is the problem.

options.title: Yes — disable GPU telemetry
options.desc: Less background service noise from vendor driver stacks.

options.title: No — keep GPU telemetry services
options.desc: Leave vendor analytics services in place.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep GPU driver telemetry services active.

desktopQuestions.label: Game DVR
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Game DVR recording overhead?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is still one of the most sensible gaming questions to ask because the action is real and the downside is clear.

options.title: Yes — disable Game DVR
options.desc: Less background recording overhead and less frame-time noise.

options.title: No — keep Game DVR behavior
options.desc: Keep built-in capture behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Game DVR behavior enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Fullscreen Optimizations
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows fullscreen optimizations?
desktopQuestions.desc: Useful for users chasing predictable fullscreen behavior and lower input-path weirdness.

options.title: Yes — disable them
options.desc: Closer to classic exclusive-fullscreen behavior in many cases.

options.title: No — keep them enabled
options.desc: Leave the Windows fullscreen layer intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows fullscreen optimizations enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Dynamic Tick
desktopQuestions.title: Disable dynamic tick for lower timer jitter?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is a proper enthusiast question pulled from the real playbook, not a marketing placeholder.
desktopQuestions.note: Best reserved for aggressive or expert profiles because it is not a universal improvement on every system.

options.title: Yes — disable dynamic tick
options.desc: Potentially tighter timing consistency on tuned desktops.

options.title: No — keep dynamic tick
options.desc: Leave Windows’ idle-saving timer behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows dynamic tick enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Edge Removal
desktopQuestions.title: Remove the Edge browser completely?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is where the tone changes from cleanup to irreversible system surgery.
desktopQuestions.note: Removing Edge is not the same thing as cleaning it up. This is one of the few questions that deserves real fear.

options.title: Yes — remove Edge
options.desc: Only for people who already know exactly why they want it gone and already have another browser installed.

warnings: Edge removal is irreversible without a manual reinstall.

options.title: No — keep the browser installed
options.desc: Stick with suppression only.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Edge browser installed.

desktopQuestions.label: WebView2
desktopQuestions.title: Preserve WebView2 for app compatibility?
desktopQuestions.desc: This question exists because too many debloat tools pretend WebView2 is “just Edge” and then people wonder why app surfaces start breaking.

options.title: Yes — preserve WebView2
options.desc: Recommended unless you know your full app stack and are intentionally stripping embedded browser runtimes.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to preserve WebView2 for app compatibility.

options.title: No — allow WebView2 removal
options.desc: Only for stripped personal systems where you explicitly accept the blast radius.

warnings: Removing WebView2 can break Teams, installer UIs, and embedded web surfaces.

desktopQuestions.label: Spectre / Mitigations
desktopQuestions.title: Reduce speculative-execution mitigations for a potential (%5-%10) performance increase?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the exact kind of question that makes or breaks credibility: the performance folklore is real enough to ask about, but the security cost is real too. In our current playbook this maps to the SSBD-side mitigation reduction path.
desktopQuestions.note: This should be benchmark-first, not dogma-first. redcore OS asks it explicitly and only applies the real mitigation action it actually has.

options.title: Yes — reduce those mitigations
options.desc: For dedicated personal rigs where you knowingly trade security for a possible latency/syscall win.

warnings: Speculation mitigation reduction trades security for latency and syscall-heavy performance.

options.title: No — keep mitigations intact
options.desc: Safer default, especially on anything used for browsing, work, or sensitive data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speculative execution mitigations intact.

desktopQuestions.label: HVCI
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Hypervisor-enforced Code Integrity (HVCI)?
desktopQuestions.desc: Another expert-only security/performance tradeoff. This is not a casual checkbox.
desktopQuestions.note: Vanguard and some hardened environments may care about this. That is exactly why the question exists.

options.title: Yes — disable HVCI
options.desc: Useful only if you understand the kernel-security tradeoff and do not rely on that baseline.

warnings: Disabling HVCI can break security baselines and some anti-cheat requirements.

options.title: No — keep HVCI enabled
options.desc: Safer and friendlier for security baselines or anti-cheat requirements.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Hypervisor-enforced Code Integrity enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: LLMNR
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Link-Local Multicast Name Resolution (LLMNR)?
desktopQuestions.desc: A sensible hardening/cleanup protocol question that many tuned systems can say yes to.

options.title: Yes — disable LLMNR
options.desc: Reduces a noisy legacy name-resolution surface.

options.title: No — keep LLMNR
options.desc: Leave local multicast name resolution behavior alone.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep LLMNR name resolution enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: IPv6
desktopQuestions.title: Disable IPv6?
desktopQuestions.desc: Classic tweak, often overused. redcore OS asks it directly instead of hiding it behind a vague “network tweak” checkbox.

options.title: Yes — disable IPv6
options.desc: Only if you know your environment does not need it.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep IPv6 enabled
options.desc: Recommended for most normal networks unless you have a reason not to.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep IPv6 enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Teredo
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Teredo tunneling?
desktopQuestions.desc: Useful if you want legacy Microsoft tunneling layers out of the way.

options.title: Yes — disable Teredo
options.desc: Less tunneling cruft in the networking stack.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep Teredo
options.desc: Leave it untouched if you are not sure.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Teredo tunneling enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: NetBIOS
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NetBIOS over TCP/IP?
desktopQuestions.desc: A very specific question, but the kind that is actually consequential on the right systems.

options.title: Yes — disable NetBIOS
options.desc: Good for stripped personal machines that do not rely on old SMB name resolution paths.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep NetBIOS
options.desc: Safer if you are not certain about your local-network dependencies.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NetBIOS over TCP/IP enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: Nagle
desktopQuestions.title: Disable the Nagle algorithm for lower packet coalescing latency?
desktopQuestions.desc: This is the kind of network question that sounds impressive and can absolutely be the wrong answer on the wrong setup.

options.title: Yes — disable Nagle
options.desc: For users chasing lower send-side latency in very specific workloads.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep default TCP behavior
options.desc: Safer for general networking behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default TCP packet coalescing behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: NIC Offloading
desktopQuestions.title: Disable NIC offloading and switch to a low-latency adapter preset?
desktopQuestions.desc: This applies the current playbook’s offloading, RSS queue, and autotuning decisions as a bundle.

options.title: Yes — use the low-latency NIC preset
options.desc: For wired enthusiast setups where throughput tradeoffs are acceptable.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.title: No — keep default NIC offloading behavior
options.desc: Safer for mixed-use, higher-throughput, or Wi-Fi-heavy machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default NIC offloading and queue behavior.

desktopQuestions.label: Delivery Optimization
desktopQuestions.title: Disable Windows Delivery Optimization bandwidth sharing?
desktopQuestions.desc: A good real-world question because it affects how Windows uses your bandwidth in the background.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Less background peer-distribution behavior from Windows Update.

options.title: No — keep it
options.desc: Leave Windows update-sharing behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Delivery Optimization enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: USB Suspend
desktopQuestions.title: Disable USB selective suspend?
desktopQuestions.desc: Useful for users chasing fewer wake-latency issues on input devices and attached hardware.

options.title: Yes — disable it
options.desc: Reduces Windows’ tendency to put USB devices to sleep.

options.title: No — keep USB selective suspend
options.desc: Safer for efficiency and some mobile setups.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep USB selective suspend enabled.

desktopQuestions.label: PCIe Link States
desktopQuestions.title: Disable PCIe link-state power management?
desktopQuestions.desc: Good enthusiast question. Specific, measurable, and not something average users think to ask.

options.title: Yes — disable PCIe link-state PM
options.desc: Cuts another latency source from the power stack on desktops.

options.title: No — keep PCIe power management
options.desc: Leave the link-state power-saving behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PCIe link-state power management enabled.

featurePages.description: This is the mainline preset. It decides how much of the playbook is eligible before your per-question answers start forcing things in or out.

options.name: aggressionPreset__conservative
options.desc: Focus on cleanup, basic privacy reduction, and low-risk ergonomics. Leaves the deeper scheduler, power, and protocol questions out.

options.name: aggressionPreset__balanced
options.desc: The serious default. Enables meaningful performance and privacy questions without drifting into enthusiast-only territory.

options.name: aggressionPreset__aggressive
options.desc: Unlocks deeper latency, networking, and power-management questions meant for tuned personal rigs.

options.name: aggressionPreset__expert
options.desc: Unlocks mitigation and HVCI questions along with the highest-risk browser/network/security tradeoffs.

featurePages.description: This forces Windows away from the default power-saving bias so the CPU stops dipping as aggressively between bursts.

options.name: highPerformancePlan__true
options.desc: Better responsiveness and less ramp-up delay under game and desktop load.

options.name: highPerformancePlan__false
options.desc: Safer for mixed-use or battery-conscious machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows balanced power behavior.

featurePages.description: This maps to Win32PrioritySeparation. It pushes the active foreground workload ahead of background noise.

options.text: Yes — bias CPU time toward the active app
options.name: optimizeThreadPriority__true
options.desc: Useful for games and latency-sensitive foreground work.

options.name: optimizeThreadPriority__false
options.desc: Better if you prefer untouched desktop multitasking behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default Windows thread scheduling behavior.

featurePages.description: This is the real Windows 11 timer question redcore OS can honestly apply: whether the system should honor global timer-resolution behavior again.

options.name: globalTimerResolution__true
options.desc: Lets one process' timer request benefit the whole system again.

options.text: No — keep modern per-process timer behavior
options.name: globalTimerResolution__false
options.desc: More power-efficient and closer to stock Windows scheduling.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep modern per-process timer behavior.

featurePages.description: Indexing keeps background I/O and CPU pressure around so Start and Explorer search stay fast. Turning it off trades convenience for quietness.

options.name: disableIndexing__true
options.desc: Reduces background disk churn and CPU spikes from the indexer.

options.name: disableIndexing__false
options.desc: Better if you actively use Start or file search.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search indexing enabled.

featurePages.description: This cuts the Microsoft content layer around Search without pretending to fully remove SearchHost.

options.text: Yes — shrink Search to a local-only utility
options.name: stripSearchWebNoise__true
options.desc: Cleaner Start/Search surface with less suggestion spam and history noise.

options.name: stripSearchWebNoise__false
options.desc: Best if you like built-in web suggestions or richer search panels.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows Search web features and history behavior.

featurePages.description: If yes, redcore OS will preserve the Print Spooler. If no, it will cut it.

options.name: keepPrinterSupport__true
options.desc: Keeps the Print Spooler and avoids surprise printer breakage.

behavior.blockReason: You said this machine still needs printer support.

options.text: No — safe to disable printing services
options.name: keepPrinterSupport__false
options.desc: Good for stripped gaming machines with no local or network printers.

featurePages.description: If yes, redcore OS preserves remote-access related services. If no, it trims that attack surface.

options.name: keepRemoteAccess__true
options.desc: Safer for workstations, remote support, or self-hosted remote access workflows.

behavior.blockReason: You said this machine still needs remote access or remote support.

options.name: keepRemoteAccess__false
options.desc: Good for personal machines that should never accept inbound remote control.

featurePages.description: This question stays on the honest side: preload, nags, and updates are separable; full browser removal is asked separately.

options.name: edgeBehavior__keep
options.desc: No preload, nag, or update suppression.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Microsoft Edge behavior unchanged.

options.text: Suppress background preload and default-browser nags
options.name: edgeBehavior__suppress
options.desc: Cuts startup boost and self-promotion without freezing browser updates.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to freeze Edge updates.

options.text: Suppress Edge and also freeze auto-updates
options.name: edgeBehavior__suppress-and-freeze
options.desc: Adds update suppression on top of preload/nag cleanup. Highest maintenance burden short of full removal.

featurePages.description: This removes the Copilot taskbar surface and its associated shell noise.

options.text: Yes — remove Copilot from the shell
options.name: disableCopilot__true
options.desc: Cleaner taskbar and less AI-first push from Windows.

options.name: disableCopilot__false
options.desc: Leave the Copilot surface intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Copilot available.

featurePages.description: Recall is one of the easiest “yes” questions in the whole flow unless you explicitly want AI timeline snapshots.

options.name: disableRecall__true
options.desc: Cuts screenshot-history style AI capture behavior entirely.

options.name: disableRecall__false
options.desc: Only sensible if you actually intend to use it.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Recall available.

featurePages.description: This trims another Windows 11 AI shell surface that most tuned machines do not need.

options.name: disableClickToDo__true
options.desc: Less shell clutter and fewer AI-driven hooks.

options.name: disableClickToDo__false
options.desc: Leave the new interaction surface intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Click to Do available.

featurePages.description: This cuts the bundled AI surfaces in Microsoft apps rather than only the headline shell features.

options.name: disableAiApps__true
options.desc: Removes AI extras from the apps most people never asked to become assistants.

options.name: disableAiApps__false
options.desc: Leave the AI tooling in those apps available.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep AI features in system apps.

featurePages.description: This controls the real telemetry actions in the playbook rather than vague “privacy mode” marketing.

options.name: telemetryLevel__keep
options.desc: Do not apply telemetry-specific privacy cuts here.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to reduce Microsoft telemetry in this area.

options.name: telemetryLevel__reduce
options.desc: Disable telemetry, CEIP, and error reporting without going all the way into the aggressive feedback cuts.

behavior.blockReason: You chose the lighter telemetry reduction profile.

options.name: telemetryLevel__aggressive
options.desc: Adds deeper feedback suppression for users who want Microsoft’s soft collection surfaces cut harder.

featurePages.description: Useful if you want the clipboard to behave like a simple local buffer again.

options.name: disableClipboardHistory__true
options.desc: Cuts cloud-linked clipboard behavior and local history retention.

options.name: disableClipboardHistory__false
options.desc: Leave clipboard convenience features enabled.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep clipboard history and sync capability.

featurePages.description: This trims the timeline-style “what you were doing” surfaces and associated cloud content behavior.

options.name: disableActivityFeed__true
options.desc: Less behavior tracking and less cloud-fed content inside Windows.

options.name: disableActivityFeed__false
options.desc: Leave Windows activity and cloud-content behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep activity feed or cloud content behavior.

featurePages.description: A good privacy answer for desktops. A more situational one for laptops that actually use location-based workflows.

options.name: disableLocation__true
options.desc: Reduces location tracking and disables Find My Device dependence.

options.name: disableLocation__false
options.desc: Better if you use maps, recovery, or location-aware apps.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows location-aware features.

featurePages.description: This removes the “Windows learns from you” layer instead of only the obvious ad toggles.

options.name: disableTailoredExperiences__true
options.desc: Less suggestion spam and less behavior-based tuning from Windows.

options.name: disableTailoredExperiences__false
options.desc: Leave Windows’ softer personalization layer intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep tailored experiences and app-launch personalization.

featurePages.description: This cuts cloud speech/input collection behavior that most stripped systems do not need.

options.name: disableSpeechPersonalization__true
options.desc: Reduces speech/input data collection and personalization layers.

options.name: disableSpeechPersonalization__false
options.desc: Better if you rely on cloud speech features or dictation behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speech and input personalization features.

featurePages.description: This is a real tradeoff question. It reduces Windows friction, but it also removes one of the last browser/download guardrails people forget they were relying on.

options.name: disableSmartScreen__true
options.desc: For stripped personal rigs where you intentionally manage trust yourself.

warnings: Disabling SmartScreen can remove a useful download and reputation safety check.

options.name: disableSmartScreen__false
options.desc: Safer for mixed-use, browsing, and non-lab machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep SmartScreen protection enabled.

featurePages.description: Fast Startup often leaves stale driver/kernel state around. Tuned systems usually benefit from clean boots.

options.name: disableFastStartup__true
options.desc: Cleaner shutdown/boot behavior and fewer weird resume-state driver bugs.

options.name: disableFastStartup__false
options.desc: Faster boots, but more hybrid-shutdown baggage.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Fast Startup enabled.

featurePages.description: For most tuned desktops this is a yes. For laptops it depends on whether you actually use hibernate.

options.name: disableHibernation__true
options.desc: Frees disk space and keeps boot behavior cleaner.

options.name: disableHibernation__false
options.desc: Leave hibernate available as a power feature.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep hibernation enabled.

featurePages.description: DSP and enhancement layers are often needless overhead unless you intentionally rely on them.

options.name: disableAudioEnhancements__true
options.desc: Cleaner, more predictable audio path with less vendor/DSP interference.

warnings: Audio enhancements off can change loudness, EQ, and motherboard vendor DSP behavior.

options.name: disableAudioEnhancements__false
options.desc: Better if you rely on loudness EQ, virtual surround, or motherboard DSP effects.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows audio enhancements enabled.

featurePages.description: This helps users who want a cleaner low-latency audio path and are okay with apps taking exclusive control of the device.

options.name: enableAudioExclusiveMode__true
options.desc: Better for low-latency or focused audio paths when the app supports it.

options.text: No — keep the current shared-mode behavior
options.name: enableAudioExclusiveMode__false
options.desc: Safer if you constantly juggle multiple apps using the same device.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to enable exclusive-mode audio control.

featurePages.description: A pure usability question, but one with a real apply-side effect.

options.name: restoreClassicContextMenu__true
options.desc: Gets rid of the compact extra-click Windows 11 context menu.

options.name: restoreClassicContextMenu__false
options.desc: Leave the modern compact menu in place.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default Windows 11 compact context menu.

featurePages.description: This is the exact Windows 11 usability tweak you asked for: kill hung apps straight from taskbar right-click without opening Task Manager.

options.name: enableEndTask__true
options.desc: Cleaner emergency-kill path for frozen apps.

options.text: No — keep default taskbar right-click behavior
options.name: enableEndTask__false
options.desc: Leave the standard Windows 11 menu intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default taskbar right-click behavior.

featurePages.description: This is a real resource-control question. It matters more than a lot of flashy “FPS tweaks.”

options.name: disableBackgroundApps__true
options.desc: Cuts silent background UWP/app activity.

options.name: disableBackgroundApps__false
options.desc: Better if you rely on synced app behavior or background refresh.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep background app execution enabled.

featurePages.description: This trades background housekeeping for a quieter machine. Good for dedicated tuned PCs; less good for people who want Windows to self-maintain.

options.name: disableAutomaticMaintenance__true
options.desc: Fewer surprise background maintenance runs while idle.

options.name: disableAutomaticMaintenance__false
options.desc: Safer if you want Windows doing its normal housekeeping.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows automatic maintenance enabled.

featurePages.description: This is the lightweight Windows-native gaming toggle, not a magical optimization. But if you game, it still belongs in the questionnaire.

options.name: enableGameMode__true
options.desc: Lets Windows bias scheduling a bit more around games.

options.text: No — keep current Game Mode behavior
options.name: enableGameMode__false
options.desc: Leave Windows to its current default behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to force Game Mode on.

featurePages.description: Small tweak, but real. Cuts acrylic/blur visual noise and a bit of compositor overhead.

options.name: disableTransparency__true
options.desc: Cleaner, flatter shell with fewer translucent effects.

options.name: disableTransparency__false
options.desc: Leave the Windows visual style intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows transparency effects enabled.

featurePages.description: This keeps the CPU pushing harder under load instead of backing off into friendlier boost policy behavior.

options.name: aggressiveBoostMode__true
options.desc: Higher performance bias and quicker sustained turbo behavior.

options.name: aggressiveBoostMode__false
options.desc: Safer thermally and calmer for mixed-use machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to force aggressive CPU boost behavior.

featurePages.description: This is the “don’t dip clocks between bursts” question. It is one of the cleaner CPU responsiveness levers in the current playbook.

options.name: minProcessorState100__true
options.desc: Reduces clock-drop latency between bursts.

options.text: No — allow Windows to downscale more freely
options.name: minProcessorState100__false
options.desc: Better for thermals and general-purpose efficiency.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to lock minimum processor state to 100%.

featurePages.description: Classic enthusiast latency question. Useful on many desktop gaming systems, less sensible on weaker or office-centric machines.

options.name: disableCoreParking__true
options.desc: Reduces wake-up latency when Windows suddenly needs more threads.

options.name: disableCoreParking__false
options.desc: Better for efficiency-biased or cooler-running setups.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep CPU core parking behavior.

featurePages.description: This hardens the scheduler profile used for Games under Windows’ multimedia scheduling system.

options.text: Yes — apply the tuned gaming profile
options.name: gamingMmcss__true
options.desc: Useful for keeping game/media threads favored over background noise.

options.name: gamingMmcss__false
options.desc: Leave multimedia scheduling closer to stock.

behavior.blockReason: You chose not to harden the MMCSS gaming profile.

featurePages.description: A sensible question on machines with enough RAM. On RAM-starved systems, the answer can be different.

options.name: disableMemoryCompression__true
options.desc: Less CPU spent compressing memory pages; best on healthy-RAM systems.

options.name: disableMemoryCompression__false
options.desc: Safer if you want Windows to defend capacity under pressure.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows memory compression enabled.

featurePages.description: HAGS is system-specific. The point here is not blind faith, it is making the choice explicit.

options.name: disableHags__true
options.desc: Often chosen for steadier behavior on tuned systems.

options.text: No — keep HAGS at the Windows default
options.name: disableHags__false
options.desc: Leave GPU scheduling behavior untouched.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep HAGS at the Windows default.

featurePages.description: Cleans out the always-on driver analytics layer without pretending the GPU driver itself is the problem.

options.name: disableGpuTelemetry__true
options.desc: Less background service noise from vendor driver stacks.

options.name: disableGpuTelemetry__false
options.desc: Leave vendor analytics services in place.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep GPU driver telemetry services active.

featurePages.description: This is still one of the most sensible gaming questions to ask because the action is real and the downside is clear.

options.name: disableGameDvr__true
options.desc: Less background recording overhead and less frame-time noise.

options.name: disableGameDvr__false
options.desc: Keep built-in capture behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Game DVR behavior enabled.

featurePages.description: Useful for users chasing predictable fullscreen behavior and lower input-path weirdness.

options.name: disableFullscreenOptimizations__true
options.desc: Closer to classic exclusive-fullscreen behavior in many cases.

options.name: disableFullscreenOptimizations__false
options.desc: Leave the Windows fullscreen layer intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows fullscreen optimizations enabled.

featurePages.description: This is a proper enthusiast question pulled from the real playbook, not a marketing placeholder.

options.name: disableDynamicTick__true
options.desc: Potentially tighter timing consistency on tuned desktops.

options.name: disableDynamicTick__false
options.desc: Leave Windows’ idle-saving timer behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Windows dynamic tick enabled.

featurePages.description: This is where the tone changes from cleanup to irreversible system surgery.

options.name: removeEdge__true
options.desc: Only for people who already know exactly why they want it gone and already have another browser installed.

warnings: Edge removal is irreversible without a manual reinstall.

options.name: removeEdge__false
options.desc: Stick with suppression only.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the Edge browser installed.

featurePages.description: This question exists because too many debloat tools pretend WebView2 is “just Edge” and then people wonder why app surfaces start breaking.

options.name: preserveWebView2__true
options.desc: Recommended unless you know your full app stack and are intentionally stripping embedded browser runtimes.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to preserve WebView2 for app compatibility.

options.name: preserveWebView2__false
options.desc: Only for stripped personal systems where you explicitly accept the blast radius.

warnings: Removing WebView2 can break Teams, installer UIs, and embedded web surfaces.

featurePages.description: This is the exact kind of question that makes or breaks credibility: the performance folklore is real enough to ask about, but the security cost is real too. In our current playbook this maps to the SSBD-side mitigation reduction path.

options.name: reduceMitigations__true
options.desc: For dedicated personal rigs where you knowingly trade security for a possible latency/syscall win.

warnings: Speculation mitigation reduction trades security for latency and syscall-heavy performance.

options.name: reduceMitigations__false
options.desc: Safer default, especially on anything used for browsing, work, or sensitive data.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep speculative execution mitigations intact.

featurePages.description: Another expert-only security/performance tradeoff. This is not a casual checkbox.

options.name: disableHvci__true
options.desc: Useful only if you understand the kernel-security tradeoff and do not rely on that baseline.

warnings: Disabling HVCI can break security baselines and some anti-cheat requirements.

options.name: disableHvci__false
options.desc: Safer and friendlier for security baselines or anti-cheat requirements.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Hypervisor-enforced Code Integrity enabled.

featurePages.description: A sensible hardening/cleanup protocol question that many tuned systems can say yes to.

options.name: disableLlmnr__true
options.desc: Reduces a noisy legacy name-resolution surface.

options.name: disableLlmnr__false
options.desc: Leave local multicast name resolution behavior alone.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep LLMNR name resolution enabled.

featurePages.description: Classic tweak, often overused. redcore OS asks it directly instead of hiding it behind a vague “network tweak” checkbox.

options.name: disableIpv6__true
options.desc: Only if you know your environment does not need it.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableIpv6__false
options.desc: Recommended for most normal networks unless you have a reason not to.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep IPv6 enabled.

featurePages.description: Useful if you want legacy Microsoft tunneling layers out of the way.

options.name: disableTeredo__true
options.desc: Less tunneling cruft in the networking stack.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableTeredo__false
options.desc: Leave it untouched if you are not sure.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Teredo tunneling enabled.

featurePages.description: A very specific question, but the kind that is actually consequential on the right systems.

options.name: disableNetbios__true
options.desc: Good for stripped personal machines that do not rely on old SMB name resolution paths.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableNetbios__false
options.desc: Safer if you are not certain about your local-network dependencies.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep NetBIOS over TCP/IP enabled.

featurePages.description: This is the kind of network question that sounds impressive and can absolutely be the wrong answer on the wrong setup.

options.name: disableNagle__true
options.desc: For users chasing lower send-side latency in very specific workloads.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.name: disableNagle__false
options.desc: Safer for general networking behavior.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep the default TCP packet coalescing behavior.

featurePages.description: This applies the current playbook’s offloading, RSS queue, and autotuning decisions as a bundle.

options.text: Yes — use the low-latency NIC preset
options.name: disableNicOffloading__true
options.desc: For wired enthusiast setups where throughput tradeoffs are acceptable.

warnings: Aggressive network tuning is hardware-sensitive. Verify games, VPNs, and voice chat after apply.

options.text: No — keep default NIC offloading behavior
options.name: disableNicOffloading__false
options.desc: Safer for mixed-use, higher-throughput, or Wi-Fi-heavy machines.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep default NIC offloading and queue behavior.

featurePages.description: A good real-world question because it affects how Windows uses your bandwidth in the background.

options.name: disableDeliveryOptimization__true
options.desc: Less background peer-distribution behavior from Windows Update.

options.name: disableDeliveryOptimization__false
options.desc: Leave Windows update-sharing behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep Delivery Optimization enabled.

featurePages.description: Useful for users chasing fewer wake-latency issues on input devices and attached hardware.

options.name: disableUsbSelectiveSuspend__true
options.desc: Reduces Windows’ tendency to put USB devices to sleep.

options.name: disableUsbSelectiveSuspend__false
options.desc: Safer for efficiency and some mobile setups.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep USB selective suspend enabled.

featurePages.description: Good enthusiast question. Specific, measurable, and not something average users think to ask.

options.name: disablePcieLinkStatePm__true
options.desc: Cuts another latency source from the power stack on desktops.

options.name: disablePcieLinkStatePm__false
options.desc: Leave the link-state power-saving behavior intact.

behavior.blockReason: You chose to keep PCIe link-state power management enabled.
